Set up cluster autoscaler and verify pods trigger new node provisioning under load
Pass or thoroughly study for AWS Solutions Architect Associate exam
outcomes: Design and provision a complete AWS architecture for a production application
Deploy, scale, and debug applications on Kubernetes confidently
Explain Kubernetes networking and RBAC to a team

phases.title: Observability, Security & SRE
phases.subtitle: Monitoring, Reliability & DevSecOps
phases.description: Senior DevOps engineers and SREs don't just build systems — they ensure those systems stay reliable, observable, and secure. This phase covers the practices that separate engineers who build things from engineers who own them in production: monitoring, alerting, incident response, SLOs, and security hardening throughout the entire stack.

sections.title: Observability Stack
sections.subtitle: Metrics, Logs & Traces
topics: The three pillars of observability: metrics (what is happening), logs (what happened), traces (why it happened)
Prometheus: metrics collection, PromQL, scrape configs, recording rules, alerting rules
Grafana: dashboards, panels, data sources, variables, alerting, provisioning as code
Grafana stack: Loki (logs), Tempo (traces), Mimir (long-term metrics) — the modern open-source stack
Node Exporter and cAdvisor: system-level and container metrics for Prometheus
kube-state-metrics: Kubernetes object metrics (deployment replicas, pod status)
AlertManager: routing alerts to Slack/PagerDuty, grouping, inhibition, silences
Distributed tracing: OpenTelemetry (the standard), Jaeger, Zipkin — trace IDs through services
Datadog: full-stack observability SaaS, APM, logs, synthetics, infrastructure maps
New Relic and Dynatrace: alternative enterprise observability platforms
AWS CloudWatch: log groups, metric filters, composite alarms, CloudWatch Insights
ELK Stack: Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana — centralized logging, index management
Structured logging: JSON logs vs plain text, correlation IDs, log levels
SLI/SLO/SLA: defining and measuring service reliability commitments
Error budgets: the concept that balances reliability with velocity

sections.title: Incident Management
sections.subtitle: Responding to Production Issues
topics: Incident severity levels: P1/P2/P3/P4 — definitions and response expectations
On-call rotation: PagerDuty, Opsgenie — escalation policies, schedules
Runbooks: documented step-by-step response procedures for known failure modes
Incident command structure: incident commander, communications lead, technical lead roles
Blameless post-mortems: 5 whys, timeline reconstruction, action items — the SRE culture
Chaos engineering: Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, Litmus — intentionally breaking things to find weaknesses
Game days: simulated incident exercises to train the team before real incidents
Communication during incidents: status pages (Statuspage.io), internal Slack bridges
Toil reduction: identifying repetitive manual work, automating it away
Change management: change freeze windows, deployment approval processes

sections.title: DevSecOps
sections.subtitle: Security Baked Into Every Stage
topics: Shift-left security: security checks in the IDE, pre-commit hooks, and CI pipeline — not just at the end
SAST (Static Application Security Testing): Semgrep, CodeQL, Bandit (Python) — scanning source code
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): OWASP ZAP — testing running applications
SCA (Software Composition Analysis): Snyk, Dependabot, npm audit — vulnerable dependencies
Container image scanning: Trivy, Grype, Snyk — scanning images before push
Kubernetes security: kube-bench (CIS benchmarks), Falco (runtime threat detection), OPA/Gatekeeper (policy enforcement)
Secrets management: HashiCorp Vault (dynamic secrets, secret leasing), AWS Secrets Manager, External Secrets Operator
OWASP Top 10: injection, broken auth, XSS, IDOR, security misconfiguration — understand each
Network segmentation: VPC security groups, Kubernetes NetworkPolicies, zero-trust principles
SSL/TLS management: cert-manager on Kubernetes, Let's Encrypt automation, certificate rotation
IAM hardening: cross-account roles, SCPs, permission boundaries, access analyzer
Compliance as Code: InSpec, OpenSCAP — automated compliance checking
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials): generating and using dependency manifests
Cloud security posture: AWS Security Hub, GCP Security Command Center, Prisma Cloud

sections.title: Platform Engineering
sections.subtitle: Building Developer Platforms
topics: Internal Developer Platform (IDP): what it is, Backstage as the developer portal
Golden paths: opinionated, pre-paved workflows that guide developers without restricting them
Self-service infrastructure: developers provision environments without tickets to ops
GitOps maturity model: from manual → scripted → IaC → GitOps → full platform
ArgoCD ApplicationSets: templating many applications from a single definition
Crossplane: Kubernetes-native cloud resource provisioning for platform teams
Policy as Code: OPA/Rego, Kyverno — enforcing org standards at the Kubernetes admission layer
Cost optimization: Kubecost for K8s cost visibility, spot instances for non-critical workloads
Developer experience (DevEx): measuring and improving the developer productivity platform

sections.title: SRE Practices
sections.subtitle: Site Reliability Engineering Principles
topics: SRE vs DevOps: the Google SRE book concepts — eliminating toil, reliability engineering
Service Level Objectives (SLOs): defining 99.9% vs 99.99% availability and what it costs
Error budget policy: stopping feature development when error budget is exhausted
Capacity planning: traffic forecasting, load testing (k6, Locust, Gatling), headroom analysis
Load testing: k6 scripts, ramp-up patterns, threshold assertions, CI integration
Reliability patterns: circuit breakers, bulkheads, retry with exponential backoff, timeouts
Graceful degradation: feature flags to disable non-critical features under load
Multi-region architecture: active-active vs active-passive, data replication strategies
Disaster recovery: RTO and RPO definitions, backup testing, cross-region failover drills
Traffic management: Istio/Envoy for weighted routing, circuit breaking, retries at mesh layer

sections.title: Advanced DevOps Topics
sections.subtitle: Staff-Level Knowledge
topics: Service mesh deep dive: Istio architecture (control plane vs data plane), mTLS everywhere
eBPF: the technology powering next-gen observability (Cilium, Pixie, Tetragon)
WebAssembly (WASM) for server-side: emerging runtime for edge deployments
Kubernetes operators: extending Kubernetes with Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
Progressive delivery: feature flags + canary + A/B testing with Flagger or Argo Rollouts
Supply chain security: SLSA framework, Sigstore/cosign for image signing, provenance
FinOps: cloud cost culture, tagging strategy, showback vs chargeback, unit economics
Platform architecture decisions: when to build vs buy, managed services vs self-hosted tradeoffs

projects: Build a full observability stack: Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + AlertManager on Kubernetes with Helm
Write a complete blameless post-mortem for a simulated production incident
Implement a DevSecOps pipeline: SAST + container scanning + secrets detection as CI gates
Set up Vault for dynamic database credentials rotation in a Kubernetes application
Conduct a load test with k6, identify the bottleneck, and fix it
outcomes: Design and operate a complete observability stack for a production Kubernetes cluster
Write SLOs, define error budgets, and enforce reliability contracts across services
Implement a DevSecOps pipeline with security gates at every stage
Lead a production incident response and run a blameless post-mortem
Director of Infrastructure / VP Engineering (Infrastructure) — $190K+ with equity

timeline.milestone: Manage a Linux server entirely from CLI, write automation scripts
Implement trunk-based development with branch protection on a real repo
Containerize a full-stack app with production-grade Dockerfiles and Docker Compose
START applying for Junior DevOps roles — deploy pipeline live to a cloud environment
Provision complete AWS infrastructure with Terraform modules, configure with Ansible
Apply for DevOps Engineer roles ($100K+) — running production workloads on Kubernetes
Senior DevOps / SRE trajectory at $140K+ — owning reliability of production systems

phases.title: Foundation
phases.subtitle: Excel, Statistics & Analytical Thinking
phases.description: Every great data analyst starts with the same three tools: Excel for quick data work, statistics for reasoning about numbers, and a structured thinking process for turning raw data into decisions. These are not beginner skills you discard later — senior analysts use all three every single day.

sections.title: Excel & Google Sheets
sections.subtitle: The Analyst's First Weapon
topics: Spreadsheet navigation: keyboard shortcuts, cell references (relative, absolute, mixed)
Core functions: SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, COUNTA, MIN, MAX, IF, IFS, SUMIF, COUNTIF, AVERAGEIF
Lookup functions: VLOOKUP, HLOOKUP, INDEX-MATCH, XLOOKUP — when to use each
Text functions: LEFT, RIGHT, MID, LEN, TRIM, CONCATENATE, TEXT, SUBSTITUTE, FIND
Date functions: TODAY, NOW, DATE, YEAR, MONTH, DAY, DATEDIF, NETWORKDAYS, EOMONTH
Logical functions: AND, OR, NOT, nested IFs, IFERROR, IFNA
Array formulas: SUMPRODUCT, multi-condition aggregations without helper columns
Data validation: dropdown lists, input restrictions, error messages for clean data entry
Conditional formatting: color scales, data bars, icon sets, custom rules
Named ranges: defining and using named ranges for readable formulas
Data cleaning in Excel: Remove Duplicates, Text to Columns, Flash Fill, Find & Replace
Sorting and filtering: multi-level sort, custom sort, advanced filter, filter by color

sections.title: Pivot Tables & Charts
sections.subtitle: Summarizing Data at Speed
topics: Pivot table fundamentals: rows, columns, values, filters — the four areas
Aggregation options: sum, count, average, max, min, % of total, running total
Grouping: grouping dates by month/quarter/year, grouping numbers into bins
Calculated fields and calculated items in pivot tables
Slicers and timelines: interactive filtering across multiple pivot tables
Pivot charts: linking charts to pivot tables, chart types for different data
Power Query (Get & Transform): importing, cleaning, and transforming data without formulas
Power Pivot: data model, relationships between tables, DAX basics
Chart fundamentals: bar, column, line, pie, scatter, combo — choosing correctly
Chart formatting: titles, axis labels, data labels, gridlines, color principles
Sparklines: in-cell mini charts for trend visualization in tables

sections.title: Statistics for Analysts
sections.subtitle: The Numbers Behind the Numbers
topics: Descriptive statistics: mean, median, mode — when each is the right measure
Spread: variance, standard deviation, range, IQR — measuring data variability
Percentiles and quartiles: understanding distributions, finding outliers with IQR
Normal distribution: bell curve, 68-95-99.7 rule, z-scores
Skewness and kurtosis: identifying non-normal distributions visually
Correlation: Pearson r, positive/negative/zero correlation, correlation matrix
Correlation vs causation: the most critical distinction for any analyst
Basic probability: independent events, conditional probability, Bayes' theorem intuition
Sampling: random sampling, sample size, sampling bias — why your survey data lies
Introduction to hypothesis testing: null hypothesis, p-value intuition (no math yet)
Simpson's paradox: why aggregated data can completely reverse a trend — real examples

sections.title: Analytical Thinking
sections.subtitle: Structuring Problems Before Touching Data
topics: The analytics process: question → data → analysis → insight → recommendation → decision
Defining the right question: SMART criteria, avoiding the XY problem in analytics
Metric decomposition: breaking a business metric into its constituent drivers
Issue trees and MECE thinking: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive frameworks
Hypothesis-driven analysis: forming a hypothesis before exploring data
Sanity checking results: does this answer make real-world sense?
Distinguishing signal from noise: statistical vs practical significance
Data literacy: understanding what data can and cannot tell you
Communicating uncertainty: how to present findings without overclaiming
Stakeholder question translation: turning a vague business ask into an analytical task

projects: Build a personal finance tracker in Excel with pivot tables, charts, and a dashboard tab
Analyze a public dataset (sports, movies, e-commerce) in Excel — find 3 insights and present them
Create an Excel sales dashboard with slicers, dynamic charts, and KPI summary cards
Clean a deliberately messy dataset using only Excel Power Query — document every step
outcomes: Build any Excel report or dashboard a manager requests without Googling
Explain the difference between correlation and causation with a real example
Identify when a pivot table result is misleading due to aggregation issues
Translate a vague business question into a specific, answerable analytical question

phases.title: SQL & Database Querying
phases.subtitle: The Core Language of Data
phases.description: SQL is the single most important technical skill for a data analyst. Every company stores data in a database, and SQL is how you get it out. Master it completely — not just SELECT statements, but window functions, CTEs, and query optimization. This skill alone gets analysts hired.

sections.title: SQL Fundamentals
sections.subtitle: Querying Data from Day One
topics: Database concepts: tables, rows, columns, primary keys, foreign keys, schema
SELECT statement: selecting columns, aliasing with AS, SELECT DISTINCT
FROM and WHERE: filtering rows, comparison operators (=, !=, >, <, >=, <=)
Logical operators: AND, OR, NOT — operator precedence with parentheses
NULL handling: IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, COALESCE, NULLIF — why NULL is not zero
BETWEEN, IN, LIKE: range filters, list filters, pattern matching with wildcards
ORDER BY: ascending/descending, multi-column sorting, NULL ordering
LIMIT and OFFSET: pagination, sampling top N rows
Arithmetic in SELECT: calculated columns, integer vs float division
String functions: UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, TRIM, REPLACE, SUBSTRING, CONCAT, LIKE patterns
Date functions: CURRENT_DATE, EXTRACT, DATE_TRUNC, DATE_ADD, DATEDIFF — per database dialect
CASE WHEN: conditional logic in SQL, CASE as a bucketing tool

sections.title: Aggregation & Grouping
sections.subtitle: Summarizing Millions of Rows
topics: Aggregate functions: COUNT(*), COUNT(column), SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
GROUP BY: grouping by one or multiple columns, grouping by expressions
HAVING: filtering after aggregation — the difference between WHERE and HAVING
COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) vs COUNT(DISTINCT column): knowing which to use
Conditional aggregation: SUM(CASE WHEN ...), COUNT(CASE WHEN ...) — pivoting in SQL
GROUP BY with ROLLUP and CUBE: subtotals and grand totals
GROUPING SETS: multiple grouping levels in a single query
Filtering aggregated results: combining WHERE and HAVING in the same query
Rounding and formatting: ROUND, CEIL, FLOOR, CAST, FORMAT for clean output

sections.title: Joins & Relationships
sections.subtitle: Combining Data Across Tables
topics: INNER JOIN: only matching rows from both tables — the most common join
LEFT JOIN: all rows from left table, NULLs where no match on right
RIGHT JOIN: all rows from right table (use LEFT JOIN with tables swapped instead)
FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both tables, NULLs where no match on either side
CROSS JOIN: Cartesian product — every row meets every row, rare but useful
SELF JOIN: joining a table to itself for hierarchical or comparison queries
Join conditions: ON clause, joining on multiple columns, non-equi joins
Joining more than 2 tables: chain joins, intermediate result sets
Anti-join pattern: LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right.id IS NULL — finding unmatched rows
Duplicate rows from joins: when and why joins multiply rows, using DISTINCT
Performance of joins: indexes on join columns, join order in query planner

sections.title: Advanced SQL
sections.subtitle: Window Functions, CTEs & Optimization
topics: Subqueries: scalar subqueries, correlated subqueries, subqueries in FROM and WHERE
CTEs (Common Table Expressions): WITH clause, chaining multiple CTEs, readability
Recursive CTEs: hierarchical data (org charts, category trees) — understand conceptually
UNION and UNION ALL: stacking result sets vertically, eliminating vs keeping duplicates
INTERSECT and EXCEPT: set operations for finding overlap and differences
Window functions overview: OVER() clause, PARTITION BY, ORDER BY within window
Ranking functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), NTILE(n)
Offset functions: LAG(), LEAD() — comparing a row to the previous or next row
Aggregate window functions: SUM() OVER, AVG() OVER, COUNT() OVER, running totals
FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE(): accessing boundary values within a partition
Frame clauses: ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
Query execution order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT
Query optimization: EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE, index usage, avoiding SELECT *
Indexes: what they are, B-tree index, when they help and when they hurt writes
Common performance pitfalls: functions on indexed columns, wildcard at start of LIKE

sections.title: SQL in the Real World
sections.subtitle: Cloud Databases & Analytical Warehouses
topics: PostgreSQL: setup, psql CLI, pgAdmin — the standard open-source relational DB
MySQL / MariaDB: differences from PostgreSQL, common in web apps
Google BigQuery: serverless analytics warehouse, partitioned tables, clustering, cost control
Amazon Redshift: MPP warehouse, distribution keys, sort keys, VACUUM
Snowflake: virtual warehouses, time travel, zero-copy cloning, share data
DuckDB: in-process analytics on local files — great for learning and prototyping
SQL dialect differences: date functions, string functions, window frame defaults per DB
Writing maintainable SQL: formatting conventions, commenting, aliases, CTE naming
Version controlling SQL: storing queries in Git, dbt for production SQL workflows

projects: Analyze the full Northwind or AdventureWorks database — answer 20 business questions with SQL
Solve 50+ LeetCode/StrataScratch SQL problems (focus on window functions and CTEs)
Write a cohort retention analysis entirely in SQL using window functions and CTEs
Build a customer segmentation query using RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) scoring in SQL
outcomes: Write any SQL query including complex window functions and multi-CTE pipelines
Optimize a slow query by reading EXPLAIN output and adding the right index
Answer any business question against a relational database in under 10 minutes
Pass SQL interview rounds at top tech companies (StrataScratch Hard difficulty)

phases.title: Data Visualization & BI Tools
phases.subtitle: Tableau, Power BI & Storytelling with Data
phases.description: Data only creates value when decision-makers understand it. Visualization is how you make that happen. Tableau and Power BI are the two dominant BI tools in the industry — mastering one deeply and understanding the other broadly is what transforms you from someone who analyzes data to someone who influences decisions.

sections.title: Data Visualization Principles
sections.subtitle: Design Before Software
topics: Why most charts lie: truncated axes, cherry-picked time ranges, misleading scales
Chart selection framework: comparisons → bar/column, trends → line, part-to-whole → stacked bar or treemap, distribution → histogram/box, relationship → scatter
When NOT to use pie charts: more than 4 slices, similar-sized slices, always have an alternative
Pre-attentive attributes: color, size, position, shape — what the eye sees instantly
Color principles: sequential palettes for ordered data, diverging for above/below average, qualitative for categories, colorblind-safe palettes
Data-ink ratio: Edward Tufte's principle — remove every element that doesn't carry information
Chartjunk: 3D charts, unnecessary gridlines, decorative images — what to always avoid
The dashboard design hierarchy: most important metric first, supporting context second, detail last
Gestalt principles: proximity, similarity, enclosure, continuity — grouping visual elements
Annotation: titles that state the insight (not just the topic), callout boxes, reference lines

sections.title: Tableau
sections.subtitle: The Industry Standard BI Platform
topics: Tableau ecosystem: Tableau Desktop, Tableau Public, Tableau Server, Tableau Cloud
Connecting to data: live vs extract connections, data source filters, custom SQL
Dimensions vs measures: the blue/green distinction, discrete vs continuous
Marks card: color, size, label, detail, tooltip — building any chart from first principles
Basic charts: bar, line, scatter, map, pie, area — building each from scratch
Sorting: manual sort, sort by field, sort by nested calculation
Filtering: dimension filters, measure filters, context filters, data source filters — filter order
Calculated fields: basic arithmetic, string functions, date calculations, logical IF/CASE
Table calculations: running total, percent of total, rank, moving average — scope and direction
Level of Detail (LOD) expressions: FIXED, INCLUDE, EXCLUDE — the most powerful Tableau feature
Parameters: dynamic user inputs, parameter actions for interactivity
Sets: custom subsets of dimension members, set actions
Dual axis charts: combining two measures on the same view, synchronized axes
Reference lines and bands: average lines, confidence bands, distribution marks
Dashboard design: layout containers (horizontal/vertical), fixed vs automatic sizing
Dashboard actions: filter actions, highlight actions, URL actions, set actions
Maps: geographic roles, filled maps, symbol maps, density maps, custom territories
Forecasting and trend lines: built-in statistical models, confidence intervals
Tableau Public: publishing and embedding dashboards, portfolio building

sections.title: Power BI
sections.subtitle: Microsoft's Enterprise Analytics Platform
topics: Power BI ecosystem: Power BI Desktop, Power BI Service, Power BI Mobile, Power BI Embedded
Power Query Editor: importing data, applied steps, M language basics, merging queries
Data model: fact tables, dimension tables, star schema vs snowflake schema
Relationships: cardinality (1:1, 1:many, many:many), cross-filter direction, active vs inactive
DAX fundamentals: calculated columns vs measures — the most important distinction
DAX basics: SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, DISTINCTCOUNT, DIVIDE (safe division)
DAX filter context: how measures evaluate, why the same formula returns different values
DAX CALCULATE: the most important DAX function — modifying filter context
Time intelligence: TOTALYTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, DATEADD, DATESYTD
Iterator functions: SUMX, AVERAGEX, RANKX — row-by-row evaluation
Variables in DAX: VAR ... RETURN for readable, performant measures
Visualization types: bar, line, card, KPI, matrix, slicer, decomposition tree, key influencers
Report design: themes, custom visuals (AppSource), bookmarks for guided navigation
Row-Level Security (RLS): restricting data access by user role
Power BI Service: workspaces, datasets, reports, dashboards, scheduled refresh
Power BI vs Tableau: when each wins, enterprise considerations, licensing

sections.title: Storytelling with Data
sections.subtitle: Turning Analysis into Action
topics: The analyst's communication goal: drive a specific decision, not just show data
The SCR framework: Situation → Complication → Resolution — structuring any data story
Leading with the insight: state the finding first, then show the evidence (pyramid principle)
Designing for your audience: executive summary vs analyst deep-dive vs operational report
The one-slide rule: if you can't fit the key insight on one slide, you haven't found it yet
Annotation strategy: titles that contain the conclusion, callouts for anomalies
Presenting uncertainty: confidence intervals, sample sizes, caveats — being honest without burying the lead
Handling pushback: when stakeholders disagree with the data, how to respond
Dashboard vs report vs presentation: choosing the right format for the right situation
Self-service analytics: designing dashboards that non-analysts can use without help

projects: Build a Tableau sales performance dashboard with LOD calculations, parameter controls, and dashboard actions
Recreate the same dashboard in Power BI using DAX measures and a proper star schema data model
Create a 3-slide executive presentation from a raw dataset — lead with insight, not with data
Publish 3 dashboards to Tableau Public as your public portfolio
outcomes: Build any business dashboard in Tableau or Power BI from a raw data source
Write LOD expressions in Tableau and CALCULATE measures in Power BI
Choose the right chart type for any analytical question without hesitation
Present a data finding to a non-technical audience and drive a decision

phases.title: Python for Data Analysis
phases.subtitle: Pandas, Visualization & Automation
phases.description: Python is what separates data analysts who hit a ceiling from those who grow into senior roles. It lets you automate repetitive Excel work, analyze datasets too large for spreadsheets, build custom visualizations, and eventually move toward data science. Learn it practically — every concept applied to real analytical problems.

sections.title: Python Fundamentals for Analysts
sections.subtitle: Programming Without the Computer Science Detour
topics: Python setup: Anaconda distribution, Jupyter Notebook, JupyterLab, VS Code with Jupyter
Variables and data types: int, float, str, bool, None
String operations: indexing, slicing, f-strings, .format(), common string methods
Lists: indexing, slicing, append, extend, remove, list comprehensions
Dictionaries: key-value pairs, nested dicts, .get(), .items(), .keys(), .values()
Tuples and sets: immutability, deduplication with sets
Control flow: if/elif/else, for loops, while loops, break/continue
Functions: def, parameters, return values, default arguments, *args, **kwargs
File I/O: reading and writing CSV, JSON, Excel files with built-in Python
Error handling: try/except/finally, common exceptions (ValueError, KeyError, FileNotFoundError)
Importing libraries: import, from...import, pip install, requirements.txt

sections.title: NumPy & Pandas
sections.subtitle: The Analytical Powerhouse
topics: NumPy arrays: creation, shape, dtype, indexing, slicing, broadcasting, vectorized operations
Pandas Series: creation, indexing, filtering, operations, value_counts, string accessor
Pandas DataFrame: creation from CSV/Excel/dict/SQL, info(), describe(), head(), tail()
Indexing and selection: .loc (label-based), .iloc (position-based), boolean indexing
Handling missing data: isnull(), notnull(), fillna(), dropna(), interpolate()
Data type conversion: astype(), pd.to_numeric(), pd.to_datetime(), errors='coerce'
String operations: .str accessor — contains, replace, split, strip, lower, upper, extract
DateTime operations: .dt accessor — year, month, day, dayofweek, to_period, resample
Sorting: sort_values(), sort_index(), ascending/descending, na_position
Filtering and querying: boolean masks, .query() method, multiple conditions
Creating new columns: direct assignment, apply(), map(), np.where()
GroupBy: groupby(), agg(), transform(), apply() — the split-apply-combine pattern
Aggregation: sum, mean, count, nunique, min, max, first, last, custom functions
Merging: merge() with how parameter (inner/left/right/outer), on/left_on/right_on
Concatenation: concat() for stacking DataFrames vertically and horizontally
Reshaping: pivot_table(), melt(), stack(), unstack(), crosstab()
Window functions: rolling(), expanding(), ewm() for time series analytics
Performance: vectorized operations vs apply() vs loops — always prefer vectorized

sections.title: Data Visualization with Python
sections.subtitle: Matplotlib, Seaborn & Plotly
topics: Matplotlib fundamentals: Figure, Axes, pyplot interface, object-oriented interface
Basic plots: line, bar, horizontal bar, scatter, histogram, pie
Customization: titles, labels, legends, colors, markers, linestyles, figure size, DPI
Subplots: plt.subplots(nrows, ncols), sharing axes, tight_layout, constrained_layout
Seaborn: statistical visualization library, themes, color palettes
Seaborn chart types: histplot, boxplot, violinplot, scatterplot, lineplot, heatmap, pairplot
Seaborn FacetGrid: small multiples — same chart across subgroups
Plotly Express: interactive charts in one line, hover data, animation_frame
Plotly Graph Objects: full control, building custom interactive charts
Plotly subplots: make_subplots(), shared axes, secondary y-axis
Exporting: saving figures as PNG/SVG/PDF, Plotly HTML exports for sharing

sections.title: Analytics Automation with Python
sections.subtitle: Eliminating Repetitive Work
topics: Reading and writing Excel with openpyxl and xlsxwriter: formatting, multiple sheets, charts
Automating Excel reports: generating styled Excel files programmatically from data
PDF report generation: ReportLab, pdfplumber for extracting data from PDFs
Web scraping basics: requests + BeautifulSoup for extracting public data tables
API data extraction: requests library, JSON parsing, pagination, authentication headers
Scheduling automation: cron (Linux/Mac), Task Scheduler (Windows), scheduling Python scripts
Email automation: smtplib — sending automated report emails with attachments
Google Sheets API: reading and writing Google Sheets with gspread
Parameterized notebooks: Papermill — running the same notebook with different inputs
Streamlit: turning a Python analysis into an interactive web app in under an hour

projects: Automate a monthly Excel report: Python script reads raw data, cleans it, outputs a formatted Excel file with charts
Scrape a public data source (job postings, product prices, sports stats) and analyze trends
Build a Streamlit app that lets a user upload a CSV and get instant summary statistics and charts
Replicate a full SQL cohort analysis in Pandas on a 1M+ row dataset
outcomes: Automate any repetitive Excel or reporting task with Python
Analyze a dataset with millions of rows that Excel cannot handle
Build an interactive web app to share analysis with non-technical stakeholders
Connect to any REST API and pull data for analysis

phases.title: Business Analytics & Metrics
phases.subtitle: Product, Marketing & Financial Analytics
phases.description: Knowing SQL and Tableau is not enough to be a great analyst — you need to understand the business. This phase covers the analytical frameworks, metrics, and domain knowledge that make analysts indispensable to product, marketing, and finance teams. This is what gets you promoted.

sections.title: Product Analytics
sections.subtitle: Measuring What Users Actually Do
topics: DAU / MAU / WAU: daily, monthly, weekly active users — definition and measurement nuances
Stickiness ratio: DAU/MAU — what a good ratio looks like per product category
Retention rate: Day 1, Day 7, Day 30 retention — the most important product metric
Churn rate: monthly/annual churn, voluntary vs involuntary, leading indicators of churn
Cohort analysis: grouping users by acquisition date, building retention curves in SQL and Python
Funnel analysis: conversion rates at each stage, drop-off identification, funnel visualization
Session analytics: session length, pages per session, bounce rate, scroll depth
Feature adoption: % of users using a feature, adoption curve, power users vs casual users
North Star Metric: the single metric that best captures the product's value delivery
Engagement metrics: L28 (days active in last 28), product habits, notification opt-in rates
User journey mapping: understanding the sequence of actions leading to conversion or churn
Segmentation: analyzing metrics by cohort, geography, device, plan tier, acquisition source

sections.title: A/B Testing & Experimentation
sections.subtitle: Making Data-Driven Decisions Rigorously
topics: Why A/B testing: removing opinion from product decisions, the cost of shipping the wrong thing
Experiment design: control group, treatment group, randomization unit (user vs session vs device)
Sample size calculation: statistical power (80%), significance level (5%), minimum detectable effect
Online calculators and Python: scipy.stats for sample size, statsmodels for power analysis
Running the experiment: holdout period, no peeking rule, guardrail metrics
Statistical significance: p-value interpretation, confidence intervals, not just p < 0.05
Practical significance: a 0.1% lift may be statistically significant but not worth shipping
Type I error (false positive): incorrectly declaring a winner — inflated by peeking
Type II error (false negative): missing a real effect due to insufficient sample size
Multiple testing correction: Bonferroni correction, FDR — when running many variants
Novelty effect: users behaving differently because something is new, not because it's better
Network effects in experiments: when user A's treatment affects user B's behavior (spillover)
Bayesian A/B testing: posterior distributions, probability of being best, no fixed sample size
Post-experiment analysis: segmenting results, heterogeneous treatment effects, long-term impact

sections.title: Marketing Analytics
sections.subtitle: Measuring the Impact of Growth
topics: Marketing funnel: awareness → consideration → acquisition → activation → retention → revenue → referral
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC): total marketing spend / new customers acquired
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV/LTV): average revenue per user × average customer lifespan
